a: Loaded the CT bone mesh and normalized it (recentred to origin, converted to mm).
Built an anatomical coordinate frame with PCA — the long axis is aligned to the bone shaft.
Sampled the cortical surface and rejected any point with wall thickness below 1.5 mm.
Farthest-point-selected 4 well-spread anchors that bracket the fracture (cortical 11.8–79.1 mm).
Produced by the geometric heuristic (PCA + curvature) rung · confidence 0.65.
b: Inferred the implant family from the inputs: diaphyseal fracture + surface anchors → locking compression plate.
Selected a REAL locking compression plate (96 × 8 × 3 mm) from the implant library — not generated from scratch.
Registered it rigidly onto the plan: long axis along the shaft, seating face on the cortical surface at the fracture.
Preserved the real CAD geometry — rotation + translation only; watertight, volume unchanged.
c: Ran 3-point-bending FEA (full_fea) on the placed implant under the walking load (700 N).
Peak von Mises 1142 MPa vs yield 830 MPa → factor of safety 0.7×.
Fatigue at risk vs the endurance limit; stress-shielding index 0.91.
Verdict: FAIL — the mini-fragment plate is under-built for a femur load.
